title: Suffering for Righteousness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-14-24 -AM- Suffering for Righteousness - 1 Peter 2_18-25 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Addressing the Slavery Objection
subpoints: More slaves exist today worldwide than at any point in history
We tend to read 'slavery' through the lens of American chattel slavery — a uniquely cruel institution
First-century slavery was different: slaves could be doctors, teachers, nurses; manumission was common
This passage does not advocate for owning slaves; it addresses those who have no immediate choice
God regulates slavery (as He does divorce) because of the hardness of human hearts — not because it is His design
Paul encourages masters to free slaves and slaves to seek freedom when possible (Philemon; 1 Cor. 7:21)

outline.title: The Main Point: Serve the Lord Faithfully in Whatever Providence Places You
subpoints: Christianity is not a revolutionary movement to overthrow institutions, but it transforms societies by freeing people from self
More than half the Roman Empire were slaves; the church included them, often in leadership
Unjust suffering is the universal Christian experience — this passage speaks to all believers
Peter is not perpetuating slavery; he is telling those with no choice: be Christlike in that situation

outline.title: Trust in Christ Whatever Providence Places — Enduring Unjust Suffering (vv. 18–20)
subpoints: Submit to masters whether kind or harsh — submission does not depend on the character of the master
'All fear' in v. 18 is fear of God, not the master (cf. v. 17: 'fear God')
Being able to endure grief from unjust suffering is evidence of God's grace in our lives
Endurance is not stoic self-motivation but trust in God's awareness and care
The confidence that God will ultimately right all wrongs enables submission without resentment or bitterness
Suffering for wrongdoing earns no credit; unjust suffering endured patiently glorifies God

outline.title: Christ as Both Example and Savior (vv. 21–24)
subpoints: We are called not merely to suffering but to be Christians — in all times, including under unjust suffering
Christ is our example in how to respond to unjust suffering — but He is far more than an example
'What would Jesus do?' only makes sense in light of 'What did Jesus do?'
Christ 'committed no sin, nor was deceit found in His mouth' — the sinless One suffered for sinners
When reviled, He did not revile in return; when threatened, He committed Himself to the Father who judges righteously
He bore our sins in His own body on the cross — healing our souls by His stripes (cf. 2 Cor. 5:21)

outline.title: Application and Takeaways
subpoints: The Bible speaks to every situation of life — including socially charged issues like slavery
Unjust suffering does not define us; we can commit it to the Lord rather than growing bitter
Never define yourself as a victim or by your trauma — your ultimate identity is 'in Christ,' justified by His blood
Even those who have suffered abuse can respond in faith, trust, and obedience — as Christ did
Who we are in Christ enables us to endure the grief of unjust suffering; He sympathizes with us and gives grace

transcript:
## Introduction: Second of Three Passages on Submission

We are continuing our series on 1 Peter. This is the second passage in a series of three passages in the context of submission. The rest of 1 Peter is going to be about submission and self-denial, but these three passages are explicitly about submission.

Someone a couple of weeks ago asked me: are these passages in order from hardest to easiest? In my mind, it is the opposite — from easiest to hardest. The easiest is submission to civil government. The second hardest is submission to a master or employer. The hardest is for a wife to submit to her husband, and all of that has to do with proximity. The more you know someone, the harder it is to submit to them.

## Addressing the Slavery Objection

We are often embarrassed by passages like this. "The Bible teaches that it's okay to have slaves? That's absurd! We are enlightened people. Slavery is so 19th century."

Let me say three things about that.

**First**, there are more slaves today worldwide than at any time in history combined. So this is not something of the past — it is very much present.

**Second**, we tend to read anything about slavery through the lens of American chattel slavery prior to the Civil War — one of the most cruel activities in the history of humanity. I think of Sojourner Truth's description of what took place, and it makes your heart ache and your eyes well up. But that is not what first-century slavery was. In the first century, slaves were doctors, nurses, teachers. They had opportunities to gain their freedom. It was still slavery — still being owned by another person — but it was fundamentally different from what was practiced in the United States.

**Third**, does this passage actually teach that you should own slaves? Read it — and you'll see it does not. The claim that the Bible perpetuates slavery is a straw man, used by those who don't want to follow the scriptures and need a rational-sounding excuse.

### What Does the Bible Actually Teach About Slavery?

The Bible has never *instituted* slavery. When you find passages in the Law regulating what slaves and masters should do, God is *regulating a human institution* — the same way He regulates divorce. Matthew 19 shows that divorce exists not from the beginning but because of the hardness of man's heart. God sets a floor: "If you are going to do this, at least don't do *that*." The same is true with slavery. Slavery exists because of the wickedness of the human heart, and God regulates it to prevent it from getting worse.

Slavery is inconsistent with God's design for humanity. It contradicts the second great commandment — love your neighbor as yourself. It contradicts the exhortation to prefer one another in humility. How can you humble yourself before someone while treating them as property?

And though Paul and Peter regulate the master-slave relationship, Paul also encourages masters to free their slaves and slaves to pursue freedom. In Philemon, Paul writes to the slave-owner Philemon about his runaway slave Onesimus, who had been converted under Paul's ministry in Rome. Paul sends him back with a letter and says: "I could command you to do what is right" — and the implication is clear: *free Onesimus*. But instead, Paul gives Philemon the opportunity to do it freely, as the right thing. Onesimus is now a brother in Christ.

In 1 Corinthians 7:21, Paul says: "Were you called while a slave? Do not be concerned about it; but if you can be made free, rather use it." If there is a way to be free — a way that brings glory to God — pursue it.

### Christianity: Transforming, Not Overthrowing

Christianity is not a revolutionary faith in the sense of overthrowing governments and institutions. Jesus told Pilate: "My kingdom is not of this world." But everywhere Christianity goes, great changes take place — because Christianity frees people from self to love others. And that love results in slavery disappearing. It's not revolution; it's transformation.

Peter is writing to pilgrims and sojourners — those passing through this world on the way to a heavenly city. Slavery was everywhere in the first century — some historians think over 50% of the Roman Empire were slaves. Many of them were in the church. There was no way to tell by outward appearance who was a slave and who was free. That was a glorious thing in the church of Christ.

Peter is facing real life head on. Slaves are in the midst of this congregation, and they need to be instructed from the Word what it means to glorify God in their condition. The Bible does not ignore the realities of life in a fallen world. Unjust suffering was the reality of first-century existence. It is also the reality of twenty-first century Christian life. So instead of being embarrassed by this passage, be encouraged — the Bible speaks to life in the real world.

## The Main Point: Serve the Lord Faithfully in Whatever Providence Has Placed You

Peter is saying: this is where you are. You may not have chosen it. You may not be able to immediately escape it. So — be Christlike in that situation. Serve the Lord faithfully and with contentment, whatever state you are in, because Jesus gives you the grace and the power to do so. When you do that, God is glorified and the gospel is adorned.

## Trusting Christ Whatever Providence Places — Enduring Unjust Suffering (vv. 18–20)

> "Servants, be submissive to your masters with all fear, not only to the good and gentle, but also to the harsh. For this is commendable, if because of conscience toward God one endures grief, suffering wrongfully. For what credit is it if, when you are beaten for your faults, you take it patiently? But when you do good and suffer, if you take it patiently, this is commendable before God." (1 Peter 2:18–20)

Note: some commentators make much of the fact that Peter uses the word for a household servant rather than the general term for slave. But Paul consistently uses the general term in his household codes in Colossians and Ephesians — giving the same instructions. So I don't think Peter's word choice signals a significant difference here.

The direct command is: submit to your masters — whether kind and gentle or unjust and harsh. **Submission does not depend on the character of the one in authority.**

"All fear" in verse 18 is not fear of the master but fear of God. In verse 17, just above, Peter commanded all believers to "fear God." Submission is a religious exercise — a spiritual duty before God.

When we are able to endure grief because we trust in God, that is the evidence of God's grace in our lives. One commentator put it well: "It is not a stoic self-motivated tenacity which holds out against all opposition, but rather the trust in and awareness of God's presence and never-failing care, which is the key to righteous suffering. It is the confidence that God will ultimately right all wrongs which enables the Christian to submit to an unjust master without resentment, rebelliousness, self-pity, or despair."

If you get punished for doing something wrong, there is no glory in that — just take it. Suffering because you behaved badly is nothing to write home about. But when you endure the grief of suffering unjustly — when the instinct for revenge and self-protection rises within you and you lay it down before God — that is the evidence that God's grace is present. Because only the supernatural grace of God makes someone prone to revenge deny themselves and glorify God in that moment.

## Christ as Both Example and Savior (vv. 21–25)

> "For to this you were called, because Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example, that you should follow His steps: 'Who committed no sin, nor was deceit found in His mouth'; who, when He was reviled, did not revile in return; when He suffered, He did not threaten, but committed Himself to Him who judges righteously; who Himself bore our sins in His own body on the tree, that we, having died to sins, might live for righteousness — by whose stripes you were healed." (1 Peter 2:21–24)

We are called not merely to suffering but to be Christians — in good times and bad, when justly or unjustly treated. If you suffer unjustly, you are in good company. Christ suffered unjustly too.

Peter begins by speaking of Christ as our *example* — but he cannot stop there. Because Christ is so much more than an example. He cannot contain himself, and he breaks into speaking of the glory of salvation. Believers are not satisfied with Christ as example alone. If He is not our Savior, His example avails us nothing.

In the 1890s, Charles Sheldon wrote *In His Steps* — the source of the "What Would Jesus Do?" movement. The question is appropriate — but only in the context of the more fundamental question: **What did Jesus do?** That is what matters most.

And the answer is verse 24: **He bore our sins.** The sinless One was condemned in the place of sinful people. He "committed no sin, nor was deceit found in His mouth." When reviled, He did not revile in return. When threatened, He committed Himself to the Father who judges righteously. He did not resort to therapeutic anger-release or stoic self-suppression — He repeatedly and continually entrusted Himself and the situation to His Father.

As 2 Corinthians 5:21 says: "He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him."

Are you suffering unjustly for Christ's sake? Commit it to the Lord — not once, not twice, but continually and repeatedly, as your Savior did.

## Application

**The Bible speaks to all situations of life.** It does not flinch from difficult, socially charged issues like slavery.

**Unjust suffering does not define us.** We can trust it all to the Lord rather than growing bitter and hateful. Do not define yourself as an abuse victim or by your trauma. You are a Christian. You are *in Christ*. That is your ultimate identity. Justified by the blood of the Lamb — the One who died and rose again — you stand righteous before God.

**Even those who have suffered abuse can respond in faith, trust, and obedience.** The Lord Jesus was abused more than any other person. He experienced traumas that are unspeakable — and yet He responded in faith, trust, and obedience. He is able to sympathize with you.

**Who we are in Christ enables us to endure the grief of unjust suffering.** Some of you have situations that make the angels weep. But Christ equips you, gives you grace, and because He sympathizes with you, you can come before Him with confidence that He hears and will answer in the best way for His glory and your good.
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro expounds 1 Peter 2:18–25, addressing the passage about servants and masters by explaining the significant differences between first-century slavery and American chattel slavery, arguing that the Bible regulates rather than institutes slavery. The main application is that unjust suffering — universally experienced by Christians — can be endured through trust in God, following the example of Christ who not only models patient suffering but who bore our sins and healed our souls.
